title: Server theme load can overwrite a newer local user choice
reasoning: The initial getSettings request always applies the returned server theme when it resolves. If a user changes the theme before that request completes, the late server response can replace the newer local selection in both React state and localStorage. This loses the user's latest preference and can make the UI flip unexpectedly.
reproduction: Render ThemeProvider with api.getSettings delayed. Call setTheme('dark') before it resolves, then resolve getSettings with theme 'light'. The provider sets theme and localStorage back to 'light'.
recommendation: Ignore the initial server response once the user has made a local theme change, or compare request/version timestamps before applying it. Consider validating allowed theme values at the same boundary.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No tests were included for ThemeContext, and there is no evidence of a test that exercises delayed getSettings responses racing with setTheme.
suggestedRegressionTest: Mock api.getSettings as a deferred promise, render ThemeProvider, invoke setTheme with a different value before resolving getSettings, then assert the user's newer value remains in context and localStorage.
minimumFixScope: Update src/context/ThemeContext.jsx to guard the mount getSettings response against overwriting a more recent setTheme call.
